navigator.name: James Cook
navigator.bio: Royal Navy officer, navigator and cartographer. His first Pacific voyage (1768-1771) in HM Bark Endeavour observed the Transit of Venus from Tahiti and charted New Zealand and eastern Australia.

voyage.title: The First Voyage of Captain James Cook (1768-1771)
voyage.sponsor: Royal Society (Transit of Venus observation) & the Admiralty, under secret instructions from King George III
voyage.purpose: Observe the 1769 Transit of Venus from Tahiti for the Royal Society, then search the South Pacific under secret Admiralty instructions for the supposed southern continent — charting New Zealand and the east coast of New Holland (Australia).
voyage.summary: Cook's first Pacific voyage: from Plymouth round Cape Horn to Tahiti to observe the Transit of Venus, then west through the Society Islands to become the first European expedition to circumnavigate and chart New Zealand.

waypoints.event: The Endeavour arrived at Madeira, where the crew took on water, wine, fresh beef, and a live bullock, before setting sail again on the night between September 18th and 19th, 1768.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: We took in 270 lb. of fresh beef, and a live bullock, charged at 613 lb. 3032 gallons of water, and ten tons of wine; and in the night, between Sunday the 18th and Monday the 19th of September, we set sail in prosecution of our voyage.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: The Endeavour arrived at Rio de Janeiro, where the crew sought to procure water and refreshments. Mr. Hicks, the first lieutenant, was sent ahead in the pinnace to inform the Governor and request a pilot to guide the ship to anchoring-ground.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: On this day we stood along the shore for Rio de Janeiro, and at nine the next morning made sail for the harbour. I then sent Mr. Hicks, my first lieutenant, before us in the pinnace, up to the city, to acquaint the Governor, that we put in there to procure water and refreshments; and to desire the assistance of a pilot to bring us into proper anchoring-ground.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Strait of Le Maire, Tierra del Fuego, Argentina
waypoints.event: The Endeavour passed through the Strait of Le Maire, making observations of the land and collecting wood and water. The crew explored the area, including landing in a bay and visiting an Indian town.
waypoints.diary_excerpt:
ON the 18th and 19th, we were delayed in getting on board our wood and
water by a swell: but on the 20th, the weather being more moderate, we
again sent the boat on shore, and Mr. Banks and Dr. Solander went in it.
They landed in the bottom of the bay; and while my people were employed
in cutting brooms, they pursued their great object, the improvement of
natural knowledge, with success, collecting many shells and plants which
hitherto have been altogether unknown: they came on board to dinner, and
afterwards went again on shore to visit an Indian town, which some of
the people had reported to lie about two miles up the country.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Ahe Atoll, Tuamotu Archipelago, French Polynesia
waypoints.event: The Endeavour sighted and named Lagoon Island, recording its position and noting the variation of the needle. The crew observed the island's features and soon after sailed onward to the west.
waypoints.diary_excerpt:
spot, which lies in latitude 18° 47ʺ S., and longitude 139° 28ʹ W. we
gave the name of LAGOON ISLAND. The variation of the needle here is 2°
54ʹ E.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: The Endeavour arrived at Otaheite (Tahiti), where rules were established for trading with the natives and the crew began interacting with local chiefs.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: THE ARRIVAL OF THE ENDEAVOUR AT OTAHEITE, CALLED BY CAPTAIN WALLIS KING GEORGE THE THIRD’S ISLAND.—RULES ESTABLISHED FOR TRAFFIC WITH THE NATIVES, AND AN ACCOUNT OF SEVERAL INCIDENTS WHICH HAPPENED IN A VISIT TO TOOTAHAH AND TOUBOURAI TAMAIDA, TWO CHIEFS.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: Cook and his crew arrived at the island of Huaheine, anchoring in a harbour on the west side called OWHARRE by the natives. They were visited by the king and his wife, and went ashore to meet the local people.
waypoints.diary_excerpt:
On the 16th, we had a gentle breeze; and in the morning about eight
o’clock, being close in with the north-west part of the island Huaheine,
we sounded, but had no bottom with 80 fathom. Some canoes very soon came
off, but the people seemed afraid, and kept at a distance till they
discovered Tupia, and then they ventured nearer. In one of the canoes
that came up to the ship’s side, was the king of the island and his
wife. Upon assurances of friendship, frequently and earnestly repeated,
their majesties and some others came on board. At first they were struck
with astonishment, and wondered at every thing that was shewn them; yet
they made no inquiries, and seeming to be satisfied with what was
offered to their notice, they made no
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: Cook and his crew arrived at Ulietea (Raiatea), anchoring in the entrance of a channel on the west side of the island to stop a leak in the powder room and to take in more ballast.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: In the afternoon we found ourselves nearly the length of the south end of Ulietea, and to windward of some harbours that lay on the west side of this island. Into one of these harbours, though we had before been ashore on the other side of the island, I intended to put, in order to stop a leak which we had sprung in the powder room, and to take in more ballast, as I found the ship too light to carry sail upon a wind. As the wind was right against us, we plied off one of the harbours, and about three o’clock in the afternoon on the 1st of August, we came to an anchor in the entrance of the channel
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: The Endeavour approached within a league of Otaha (modern Tahaa) on April 25th, 1769, observing the island and its nearby islets, and confirming the presence of a good harbour as reported by Tupia.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: At four o’clock in the afternoon of the 25th, we were within a league of Otaha, which bore N. 77 W. To the northward of the south end of that island, on the east side of it, and something more than a mile from the shore, lie two small islands, called TOAHOUTU and WHENNUAIA; between which, Tupia says, there is a channel into a very good harbour, which lies within the reef, and appearances confirmed his report.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: The Endeavour sailed close under the peak of Bolabola (Bora Bora), but found the island inaccessible on that side and was unable to weather it, requiring several tacks before passing the south end late at night.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: As soon as the boat was hoisted in, I made sail to the northward, and at eight o’clock in the morning of the 29th, we were close under the Peak of Bolabola, which was high, rude, and craggy. As the island was altogether inaccessible in this part, and we found it impossible to weather it, we tacked and stood off, then tacked again, and after many trips did not weather the south end of it till twelve o’clock at night.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Poverty Bay, Gisborne District, New Zealand
waypoints.event: Captain Cook and his crew made landfall at Poverty Bay, which he named due to its lack of provisions, and had their first significant interactions with the local Māori people. The bay was described as inhospitable, providing little of what the crew needed except some wood.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: THE next morning, at six o’clock, we weighed, and stood away from this unfortunate and inhospitable place, to which I gave the name of POVERTY BAY, and which by the natives is called TAONEROA, or Long Sand, as it did not afford us a single article that we wanted, except a little wood.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Tolaga Bay, Gisborne District, New Zealand
waypoints.event: The Endeavour anchored in Tolaga Bay, where the crew obtained water and a large supply of celery, and interacted with the local Maori inhabitants. The bay was described in detail, including its anchorage and surrounding features.
waypoints.diary_excerpt:
This bay is called by the natives TOLAGA; it is moderately large, and
has from seven to thirteen fathom, with a clean sandy bottom and good
anchorage; and is sheltered from all winds except the north-east. It
lies in latitude 38° 22ʹ S. and four leagues and a half to the north of
Gable-end Foreland. On the south point lies a small but high island, so
near the main as not to be distinguished from it. Close to the north end
of the island, at the entrance into the bay, are two high rocks; one is
round, like a corn-stack, but the other is long, and perforated in
several places, so that the openings appear like the arches of a bridge.
Within these rocks is the cove where we cut wood, and filled our
water-casks.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Mercury Bay, Coromandel Peninsula, New Zealand
waypoints.event: Cook and his crew anchored in Mercury Bay, observed the transit of Mercury, interacted with local Māori, and took formal possession of the land for Britain. They also marked a tree with the ship's name, the commander's name, and the date before departing.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: Before we left the bay, we cut upon one of the trees near the watering-place the ship’s name, and that of the commander, with the date of the year and month when we were there; and after displaying the English colours, I took a formal possession of it in the name of his Britannic Majesty King George the Third.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Bay of Islands, Northland, New Zealand
waypoints.event: Cook anchored the Endeavour in the Bay of Islands, on the south-west side of one of the many islands lining the bay, and found good anchorage and refreshment. The bay was named 'Bay of Islands' by Cook due to the great number of islands lining its shores.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: This bay, as I have before observed, lies on the west side of Cape Bret, and I named it the BAY OF ISLANDS, from the great number of islands which line its shores, and from several harbours equally safe and commodious, where there is room and depth for any number of shipping. That in which we lay is on the south-west side of the south westernmost island, called MATURARO, on the south-east side of the bay. I have made no accurate survey of this bay, being discouraged by the time it would cost me; I thought also that it was sufficient to be able to affirm that it afforded us good anchorage, and refreshment of every kind.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.event: Cook and the Endeavour sighted the Three Kings Islands on December 24th and on Christmas Day 1769, the islands bore E. 8 N. at a distance of five or six leagues as the ship tacked and stood to the southward.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: At midnight, we tacked and stood to the N. E. till six the next morning, which was Christmas-day, when we tacked and stood to the southward. At noon, the Three Kings bore E. 8 N. distant five or six leagues.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James

waypoints.place_modern: Queen Charlotte Sound, Marlborough, New Zealand
waypoints.event: Cook and his crew entered Queen Charlotte’s Sound, where they careened the ship, repaired defects, and replenished wood and water. Cook formally took possession of the inlet for King George III, naming it Queen Charlotte’s Sound and raising the Union flag.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: I then took the post to the highest part of the island, and, after fixing it firmly in the ground, I hoisted upon it the Union-flag, and honoured this inlet with the name of QUEEN CHARLOTTE’S SOUND; at the same time taking formal possession of this and the adjacent country, in the name and for the use of his Majesty King George the Third. We then drank a bottle of wine to her Majesty’s health, and gave the bottle to the old man who had attended us up the hill, and who was m
waypoints.diary_source_citation: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage. — Cook, James
